US Treasury Secretary Bessenter Urges Lawmakers to Advance the Clarity Act

On May 29, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessenter urged lawmakers to advance the Clarity Act: "Our most important task right now is to bring the digital asset industry to the United States and make the United States the home of this field. I urge both the House and Senate to complete the legislation of the Clarity Act as soon as possible."

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Tencent Cloud Database Fully Upgraded for Agents, Providing Three Major AI Native Database Capabilities

    3. US AI Server Manufacturers' Stocks Surge Pre-Market: HP Rises Nearly 5%

    On May 29, following Dell's upward revision of its profit and revenue forecasts for the current fiscal year, stocks related to AI server manufacturers surged pre-market. HP (HPQ.US) rose nearly 5%, AMD (SMCI.US) rose over 10%, and IBM rose over 5%.

      4. AI Agent Security Platform Geordie Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding, Led by Balderton Capital

The company focuses on AI Agent governance and security, helping enterprises monitor the behavior, permissions, data access, and potential risks of internal AI agents in real time, and limiting non-deterministic AI agent decision-making behavior.

        4. AI Agent Security Platform Geordie Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding, Led by Balderton Capital, with participation from Crosspoint Capital, General Catalyst, and Ten Eleven Ventures.

        On May 29th, Jademont, Founding Partner and CEO of Waterdrop Capital, posted on X, stating that at this point, the crypto industry is at its coldest, while the AI ​​industry is at its hottest. He wanted to tell some friends who were considering leaving the crypto space that these two industries—one improving productivity, the other transforming production relations—are the two legs of social progress, inseparable. Smart people can ride the wave, while ordinary people can wait patiently.

          On May 29, the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, called on regulators to allow regulated exchanges to launch 24/7 on-chain perpetual contract trading to achieve a "level playing field" with the native crypto market. ICE CEO Jeffrey Sprecher stated that regulators are currently restricting traditional exchanges from entering this area, while the relevant market already exists on-chain, and emphasized that the traditional financial system and the crypto market should be allowed to compete under the same rules. It is understood that ICE has already conducted multiple rounds of discussions with decentralized derivatives trading platforms on on-chain perpetual contract mechanisms to explore the synergy and potential integration paths between traditional finance and the crypto market.

Institutional clients can directly create and manage stVaults within their existing custody accounts, performing operations such as ETH deposits and withdrawals, stETH minting and burning, vault health monitoring, and rebalancing.

          ... This integration expands upon Cactus Custody's existing stETH and wstETH custody support, allowing institutional clients to now use Lido liquidity-staking tokens in conjunction with stVault operations.

          4. Sei Releases Giga Upgrade Roadmap, Aiming for 200,000 TPS and 400ms Finality

          On May 29th, Sei released its Giga upgrade roadmap. Giga is Sei's next-generation protocol upgrade, designed to achieve higher transaction performance by refactoring the execution client, state management, and consensus mechanisms.

          According to the roadmap, Sei will launch several core upgrades, including: Ares execution client upgrade (significantly improving performance through pipelined execution, asynchronous processing, and other optimizations); Eidos state management upgrade (replacing Merkle trees with flat key-value stores, separating hot states from cold archives); Autobahn consensus (aiming for 200,000 TPS and 400ms finality, already achieving 5 Gigagas processing capacity in an internal distributed testnet); Sedna private memory pool (introducing a cryptographic transaction propagation mechanism); and a gradual transition to an EVM-only architecture (deprecating the Cosmos SDK and CosmWasm through SIP-3). data.title: How can we enable the AI ​​Agent to call API ERC-8257 itself?
data.description:
Author: Shirley Li, Researcher at Web3Caff Research

Compared to human users, the biggest advantage of AI agents lies in their stronger autonomous execution capabilities under ideal conditions: they can complete tasks and perform operations independently, and proactively invoke external tools without continuous human intervention. However, in the actual process of AI agents invoking tools (such as trading platform APIs, data analysis tools, and oracles), some problems still arise.

First, the access points to these tools are scattered across GitHub, official websites, centralized API platforms, etc., lacking a unified discovery channel. Without human intervention, AI agents find it difficult to autonomously locate and access the required tools, and the specific payment methods of different platforms also vary, lacking standardized processes. This causes some difficulties for AI agents invoking tools.

Second, in the traditional internet, calling APIs usually requires developers to register an account, obtain an API key, and undergo permission verification according to specific rules.

This process was originally designed for human participants, but for AI agents, there is currently a lack of open and standardized implementation solutions for automatically completing registration, obtaining credentials, and invoking tools. While the x402 protocol currently supports AI agents in automatically completing payments, it is mainly applicable to open interfaces with a "pay-to-use" model and struggles to handle more complex permission scenarios, such as only subscribed users being able to access services, or only users holding certain credentials being able to enjoy discounts. To fill this gap, OpenSea recently launched a draft ERC-8257 standard, aiming to establish an open, permissionless on-chain tool catalog for AI agents, allowing them to autonomously discover tools, understand access rules, and automatically complete invocation and payment upon meeting the conditions. In short, the core of ERC-8257 is an on-chain tool registry. This registry is essentially a smart contract, allowing tool developers to register their tool's information and access permissions on the blockchain and make them publicly available. However, due to the high cost of directly uploading all data to the blockchain, ERC-8257 allows developers to store more detailed tool information on their own servers or domains, presented as a JSON-formatted manifest. The on-chain registry only records a link to this file. This off-chain file typically includes: tool name, function description, API interface, calling method, pricing information, payment protocol, access rules, etc. The on-chain registry needs to record key data such as the address of the off-chain file, the file hash value, and tool developer information. This design aims to prevent developers from subsequently tampering with the tool content. When the AI ​​Agent calls the tool, it can verify whether the off-chain content matches the information registered on the blockchain by checking the file hash value. Another crucial design feature of ERC-8257 is that access permissions are not fixed in format but defined through a separate smart contract. Tool developers can freely define this contract to specify who is qualified to call their tools. For example, developers can check whether the AI ​​Agent holds a certain NFT, a certain token, has subscribed, or is on a whitelist, etc. Consider this example: a certain on-chain analytics tool stipulates that the cost for a regular user to call a regular API is $0.05 per call, while a user holding a certain NFT only needs to pay $0.01 per call. Furthermore, if a user subscribes to its service (paying continuously via a specified token or payment protocol), they also gain access to advanced analytics interfaces. In this scenario, "holding a certain NFT" and "subscribing to a service" are two special access credentials. If the AI ​​Agent currently does not have the corresponding permissions, it can acquire these qualifications on-chain or in the marketplace (e.g., by purchasing an NFT or completing a subscription) and then reapply for the access. However, it's important to note that when access permissions exist in the form of assets such as NFTs or tokens, these assets themselves may enter the market circulation system, thus being affected by supply and demand, leading to high value fluctuations or speculative behavior. Therefore, ERC-8257 does not restrict the permission system to a single asset model, but chooses to maintain openness. Tool or service developers can choose different access mechanisms according to their specific needs. For example, they can introduce non-transferable Soulbound NFTs to avoid value fluctuations caused by transactions, or introduce non-asset-based mechanisms such as reputation scoring to reduce the impact of speculative behavior. At the payment level, ERC-8257 does not define the specific payment logic, but only requires developers to declare which payment protocols they support in the JSON file, such as x402, on-chain ERC-20 payments, or other machine payment protocols. The actual payment execution will be handled by the corresponding protocol. From an overall process perspective, ERC-8257 works roughly as follows: Tool developers deploy tool services and write corresponding access permissions, then submit the relevant information to the on-chain registry; When an AI Agent needs to call a tool or service, it can scan the on-chain registry. If it finds a tool or service that meets its needs, it can further read the detailed description file to understand the calling rules; If the AI ​​Agent does not meet the access conditions, it can try to obtain the corresponding permissions and then initiate the call again; Ultimately, the AI ​​Agent can autonomously complete the entire process of tool discovery, permission verification, payment, and invocation without human intervention.

Image source:The App Store for Agent Tools: ERC-8257

Overall, ERC-8257 attempts to solve not only the problem of how to put APIs on the blockchain, but also how AI agents can automatically discover tools, understand access rules, obtain access permissions, and call these tools in a standardized way, just like human users. From a design perspective, ERC-8257, together with the x402 protocol, will form a complementary relationship: ERC-8257 aims to enable AI Agents to discover tools globally and determine their access rights based on rules; the x402 protocol handles payment and settlement during tool invocation. Once a tool is authorized for use, AI Agents can be paid per use or based on invocation frequency. However, besides the aforementioned potential for value fluctuations and speculative risks if access rights exist in the form of assets such as NFTs or tokens, the ERC-8257 standard will face some potential risks and challenges in its practical implementation. For example, although ERC-8257 provides a standardized framework for tool registration and access, different developers still differ in setting access conditions. While AI Agents can rely on a unified on-chain index path for tool discovery, they still need to be compatible with different permission judgment logics during actual invocation, which introduces certain technical complexities. Furthermore, regarding trust mechanisms, AI Agents currently verify whether files have been tampered with during transmission by comparing the hash value recorded on-chain with the off-chain tool description file. However, this mechanism only solves the problem of data consistency and cannot further guarantee the correctness of the tool's operating logic, the trustworthiness of its interface, or the potential risks of information leakage during data processing. At the same time, since tool services are usually deployed on off-chain infrastructure, their long-term availability and stability still depend on the developer's operational capabilities. This means that AI Agents also need to rely on external reputation mechanisms for verification. Therefore, before the ERC-8257 standard is practically applied, its aspects regarding tool trustworthiness and the consistency of permission rules still need further verification and improvement.
