data.title: The escalating threat of AI is reshaping the defensive mechanisms and operating rules of the DeFi ecosystem.
data.description:
If a DeFi security professional suddenly started advising friends and family to withdraw all their money from DeFi protocols like Aave and Compound—how would you react?

This isn't panic selling. Manuel Aráoz, co-founder of OpenZeppelin, recently expressed this view publicly. His reasoning is simple: the asymmetry between offense and defense is intensifying at an unprecedented rate.

Data confirms this intuition.

In April 2025, DeFi suffered a total of $630 million in losses. Since mid-April, the total value locked (TVL) in DeFi has fallen by 14%, from $172 billion to $148 billion. This $630 million loss in a single month is the worst monthly figure since the $1.5 billion theft from Bybit in February 2025. Drift and Kelp DAO lost $285 million and $293 million respectively, both attributed to North Korean hackers. However, what Aráoz really wants to say is not "yet another hacking incident." He was referring to a fundamental structural problem—"Defenders need to fix every vulnerability, while attackers only need to exploit it once. AI is giving attackers superhuman abilities to discover vulnerabilities." This statement deserves careful consideration. Traditional software security is already an asymmetric war: defenders need to plug all vulnerabilities, while attackers only need to find one. In DeFi, this asymmetry is amplified tenfold—smart contracts are almost immutable once deployed, funds are directly exposed to on-chain logic vulnerabilities, anyone in the world can launch an attack at any time, and once successful, recovery is extremely difficult. The intervention of AI has given attackers an unprecedented weapon. This is not a hypothetical threat. By the end of 2025, research had confirmed that a compromised AI coding agent had achieved 80% to 90% autonomous network penetration against approximately 30 institutions worldwide with minimal human intervention. The era of AI-driven attacks is not "coming soon," but has already arrived. Claude Mythos may rewrite the landscape. Against this backdrop, in April 2026, Anthropic trained a new model—Claude Mythos. Independent testing by the UK-based AI Security Research Institute (AISI) yielded astonishing figures: in expert-level Capture the Flag (CTF) cybersecurity challenges, Mythos Preview achieved a 73% success rate. Prior to April 2025, no AI model could accomplish this task. Claude Mythos Capabilities Snapshot Vulnerability Discovery: Discovered 181 usable vulnerabilities in a single test, covering all major operating systems and browsers, including multi-step browser sandbox escape chains. Attack Simulation: Became the first AI model capable of fully executing a simulated "32-step enterprise network intrusion" end-to-end attack chain. Zero-Day Vulnerabilities: Autonomously discovered previously unknown software vulnerabilities, including hidden dangers left over from systems 30 years ago. Capability Threshold: Two years ago, the best AI models could barely complete beginner-level cybersecurity tasks; Mythos directly reached a level that only top human experts could match. Using Turing Award winner and world-class AI scientist Yoshua Bengio... The report states that this is "the first time in human history that AI has discovered zero-day vulnerabilities on a large scale," and these vulnerabilities could be used to attack banking systems, government networks, medical infrastructure—as well as DeFi protocols. But the story only just begins. The same knife, held by the defender. But there's another side to this story, and it's equally important. Along with launching Mythos, Anthropic initiated a defense initiative called Project Glasswing: using Mythos to proactively scan mainstream software for security vulnerabilities, finding and fixing them before attackers can exploit them. Within a month, Anthropic and 50 restricted partners identified over 10,000 high-risk vulnerabilities in mainstream software systems, covering over 1,000 open-source projects. Mozilla patched 271 vulnerabilities discovered by Mythos in a single Firefox update. Cloudflare discovered 2,000 vulnerabilities in its critical infrastructure. A participating bank used Mythos to intercept a $1.5 million fraudulent wire transfer midway—the attackers had compromised the customer's email and made spoof calls, but the AI ​​detected the deception. A more noteworthy detail: CVE-2026-5194, discovered within this vulnerability, is a critical flaw in WolfSSL, an open-source cryptographic library renowned for its security. With a CVSS score of 9.1, it allows attackers to forge TLS certificates, theoretically affecting billions of IoT devices—this vulnerability existed silently in the code before it was publicly disclosed. In other words, Mythos is not just an attack weapon; it's also currently the most powerful defense scanning tool—the only question is who will use it first. DeFi doesn't need to exit, it needs to upgrade. This logic also applies to DeFi. Past security audits relied on a combination of manual labor and tools; auditing a complex protocol could take weeks, and there were blind spots in coverage. AI auditing can scan a wider range of code in a shorter time, uncovering logical vulnerabilities that humans easily overlook. If the defending party first establishes this capability and proactively seeks out its own vulnerabilities, the passive, defensive situation will change. This is an arms race, but it's not one-sided.

Potential Value of AI Defense Systems for DeFi

  • Continuous Audit Layer: AI scans protocol code 24/7, automatically rerunning audit logic after each contract update, compressing the security response window from "weekly" to "hourly"

  • On-Chain Anomaly Monitoring: Like intercepting fraudulent wire transfers, it identifies abnormal transaction patterns and triggers circuit breakers before an attack fully unfolds

  • White Hat Capability Equalization: When AI tools are made available to community security researchers, the ability to find vulnerabilities is no longer the exclusive domain of state-owned hackers; DeFi's defenses can truly extend to the community

  • Narrative Opportunities in the Security Track: AI-driven on-chain security infrastructure is itself the next Web3 narrative worth paying attention to—who will be the first to introduce Mythos-level capabilities into DeFi?

  • Defense Systems: Who Defines the Next Generation of Security Standards? The Concern Isn't the Endgame Aráoz's concerns are real, but they describe an old paradigm crumbling, not the endgame for DeFi. What is the old paradigm? It's the "code is law" security assumption: a contract is audited once, passes code review, and is secure forever. This assumption was already riddled with flaws before the advent of AI; it's just being breached more quickly now. What is the new paradigm? Project Glasswing provides a prototype: security isn't a one-off event, but a continuously operating capability. Defenders don't need "better human audits," but rather "defense AI that can rival attacker AI." Interestingly, Anthropic itself admits that less than 1% of the vulnerabilities discovered by Glasswing have been patched. Over 23,000 vulnerabilities have been found, but the patching progress lags far behind the discovery rate. This indicates the challenge remains enormous—but it also highlights the immense market potential for security infrastructure capable of achieving this. In conclusion, every technological leap disrupts the existing balance of offense and defense. In the early days of the internet, some said "the internet is never secure," but security systems eventually caught up—because the motivation for defense was strong enough. With DeFi locking up hundreds of billions of dollars in assets, this motivation will only be stronger. The security problem of DeFi is not a "technically unsolvable deadlock," but rather that "old defense tools cannot keep up with new attack tools." The emergence of Claude Mythos proves that AI can already surpass humans in vulnerability discovery. The key question remains: who will first secure this weapon in the hands of the defenders? Glasswing has answered this question—at least in the realm of traditional internet infrastructure. The DeFi version is still waiting. Waiting for protocol developers, security agencies, DAO communities, and Web3-native security teams to implement the same logic on-chain. Those who withdrew from DeFi during the April panic weren't necessarily wrong. But those who stayed to rebuild the defense system are defining what the next era of DeFi will look like.

data.title: What happened to SpaceX's pre-market contracts?
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

On May 28th, less than a month before SpaceX's Nasdaq listing, and amidst soaring market enthusiasm, its pre-IPO assets experienced a series of "cliff-like crashes" overnight: the price of preSPCX stock tokens on Bitget plummeted by approximately 80% at one point; SpaceX perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid (a decentralized perpetual contract exchange) crashed by 45% within 30 minutes, resulting in over 400 retail investors being liquidated and forced to exit the market.

Even before the SpaceX listing bell rang, the market had already written a warning note for pre-IPO assets.

Bitget: 80% Plunge, But the Truth is a Token Split

Bitget: 80% Plunge, But the Truth is a Token Split

Yesterday, many traders glued to their computer screens gasped. The preSPCX/USDT spot trading pair on Bitget's chart suddenly plummeted by 80% with a vertical drop. Such dramatic volatility could easily lead uninformed investors to believe that the exchange was manipulating the price to fleece them.

Bitget: 80% Plunge, But the Truth is a Token Split

Bitget: 8 According to PANews' investigation, this "plummeting" price drop was not essentially due to a sudden deterioration in the asset's fundamentals; the truth was actually a pre-announced token split. According to Bitget's official announcement, the platform suspended trading of the original Pre-IPO stock token preSPAX at 2 PM on May 28th and implemented a 1:5 split, with the token code updated to preSPCX after the split. A 1:5 split means that the number of tokens held by investors is multiplied by 5. Assuming the total value of the holdings remains unchanged, the price per token after the split is reduced to one-fifth of the original, which translates to a nominal price drop of 80% on the trading screen. Based on the trading price of around $900 at the time, the adjusted price after the split is approximately $180. Although this was a simple technical adjustment on paper, after trading reopened, the candlestick charts on the trading interface did not reflect historical prices, resulting in a visually apparent "plummeting" market. For inexperienced, emotionally sensitive, or informationally disadvantaged retail investors, this visual shock might be misinterpreted as an "asset collapse."

Hyperliquid: 45% Flash Crash in 30 Minutes, Oracle Anomaly Triggers Domino Liquidation

Compared to Bitget's "technical adjustment," the flash crash of SPACEX perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid was a real disaster caused by oracle errors, thin liquidity, and leveraged panic selling.

In contrast to Bitget's "technical adjustment," the flash crash of SPACEX perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid was a real disaster caused by oracle errors, thin liquidity, and leveraged panic selling.

Last night at 11 PM, the SPACEX-USDH contract on the HIP-3 Ventuals market plummeted from $2,277 to a low of $1,254 in just 7 minutes, a drop of 45%, nearly halved. Although the price quickly recovered to around $2,200 within the next 10 minutes, the position clearing was complete, and the long positions suffered heavy losses. As a result, 1,393 positions held by 405 users were forcibly liquidated, resulting in a total nominal loss of approximately $1.51 million. The median margin of the liquidated positions was only $31, indicating that the market participants were primarily retail investors. This flash crash was caused by an abnormal liquidation due to an oracle price feed error. According to the Hyperliquid ecosystem perpetual contract platform Ventures, the incident was caused by an incorrect off-chain data provider used by the oracle component, which returned erroneous data. This caused significant fluctuations in the market oracle price and the token price, triggering the forced liquidation of some user positions. Emergency measures have been taken, and the development team is assessing the impact of the incident on users. Ventuals later updated the statement, saying affected users would receive compensation within the next 48 hours. Meanwhile, the deeper reason for this crash lies in the fact that pre-IPO assets generally suffer from insufficient order book depth and fragile market liquidity. This, coupled with the cascading collapse of retail investor leverage, further amplified the market panic and accelerated the spread of the decline. This was a rather "shallow" market, like a pond that only reached ankle-deep; even a slightly larger stone thrown in would create a ripple. In a market with less than $3 million in open interest and a 24-hour trading volume of just over $4 million, a single sell order of several hundred thousand dollars is enough to instantly wipe out a large number of buy orders; the price drops, triggering liquidation lines for long positions, resulting in a market sell-off that further breaks through deeper price support; the liquidity crunch late at night triggers a domino effect. In less than 20 minutes, a leveraged game turned into a forced exit for retail investors. Conclusion Undeniably, pre-IPO assets represent one of the most imaginative innovative directions in the crypto industry: allowing ordinary people to access the value mapping of top unicorns ahead of the high walls of the private equity market. In a sense, this expands the boundaries of market participation and provides a new price discovery mechanism for non-public assets. However, before real assets are officially listed on the public market and trading depth is fully developed, these assets are destined to be characterized by high volatility, high speculation, and distorted pricing. Due to the lack of a unified valuation benchmark, mature arbitrage mechanisms, and sufficient market depth, their prices are often more easily affected by sentiment, liquidity, and leveraged funds. The dramatic fluctuations in SpaceX's synthetic assets have once again exposed the vulnerability of pre-IPO products in the market. As more promising unicorns are brought onto the blockchain, more speculative capital will continue to flow in. Whether the pre-IPO sector can establish a more mature pricing mechanism, liquidity system, and risk control framework will remain a challenge for the industry.

data.title: Analysis of Solana's Real Economic Value
data.description:
Content Summary and Introduction:

The Real Economic Value (REV) on the Solana chain is the total fee paid by users for on-chain transactions, including in-protocol fees (base fee, priority fee, voting fee) and out-of-protocol tips (Jito tips). REV is a key metric for measuring block space demand. Since early 2024, Jito tips have dominated REV composition, but with network improvements, the proportion of in-protocol fees has increased. Solana has achieved REV comparable to Ethereum and Tron through low fees and high throughput, reaching approximately $1.4 billion in 2025, while its median weekly transaction fee has consistently remained below 1 cent, avoiding Ethereum's fee spiral of 2021. The application revenue to REV ratio has increased from 1:1 in early 2024 to nearly 5:1 in Q1 2026, indicating that Solana is becoming a platform economy, with the application layer capturing more and more value.

However, over time, the composition of REV began to change. With improvements to the Solana underlying network, more efficient priority fees, and reduced reliance on external mechanisms in the transaction packaging process, the share of Jito tips in REV has decreased. This is an important sign of maturity: more network execution needs are being handled directly through the protocol itself.

However, over time, the composition of REV began to change. As the Solana underlying network improved, priority fees became more efficient, and the transaction packaging process relied less on external mechanisms, the share of Jito tips in REV decreased. This is an important sign of maturity: more network execution needs are being handled directly through the protocol itself.

The Current State of Solana

Since the beginning of 2024, Solana has consistently ranked alongside Ethereum and Tron as one of the largest general-purpose blockchains in terms of REV.

Since the beginning of 2024, Solana has consistently ranked alongside Ethereum and Tron as one of the largest general-purpose blockchains in terms of REV.

In 2025, it generated approximately $1.4 billion in REV, surpassing the combined total of Ethereum and Tron; in the 12 months ending April 2026, it still leads both chains with approximately $510 million in REV. Solana generates substantial REV through high throughput and continuous network activity, rather than charging users expensive transaction fees. Solana's average daily transaction volume exceeded 1,000 transactions per second (TPS) in 2025, and has surpassed 1,200 TPS so far in 2026. The data from the beginning of 2026 to date still significantly exceeds that of other major blockchains such as BNB (approximately 177 TPS), Tron (approximately 127 TPS), Base (approximately 109 TPS), Arbitrum (approximately 28 TPS), and Ethereum (approximately 26 TPS). In summary, this is a comprehensive view of the Solana network: high REV is driven by low fees and high throughput, monetized through transaction volume and usage density rather than expensive transactions. These qualities make Solana an attractive place for builders, offering a vast user and revenue opportunity without requiring applications to be designed around high transaction costs. If REV measures what users pay to access the network, then application revenue measures whether businesses built on the network are truly capturing value. A healthy blockchain ecosystem should not only reward validators and stakers who maintain the network; it should also create an environment that allows applications to become sustainable businesses. In January 2024, Solana's application revenue and REV were roughly equal, each around $21 million. Since then, application revenue has steadily outpaced network REV, reaching nearly 5 times the ratio by the first quarter of 2026. This indicates that Solana is increasingly resembling a platform economy, providing low-cost execution at the underlying level while applications capture more end-user value. Solana's improvements in network performance and market structure have reduced the cost of on-chain transactions, which naturally compresses the value of each transaction. However, if lower costs lead to more use, more valuable activities, and more application-level monetization, the ecosystem can still become more valuable over time. For blockchains, the goal is not merely to maximize REV today, but to optimize sustainable network demand while allowing applications to thrive on it. Conclusion Solana, while generating substantial REV, maintains sufficiently low execution costs, enabling applications and users to continuously scale activity on the network. The long-term value of a blockchain should not be measured solely by how much users pay to the underlying infrastructure, but also by how much economic activity the underlying infrastructure enables. Solana's low transaction costs and high throughput are increasingly supporting higher application revenue and a larger share of value captured by the application layer. REV signals real network demand, while application revenue proves that businesses can build on it. These qualities make Solana one of the most powerful ecosystems for building businesses on-chain today.

data.title: Sequans Abandons Bitcoin Treasury Bet Less Than a Year After Embracing It
data.description:
French semiconductor company Sequans Communications has officially ended its Bitcoin treasury strategy less than a year after adopting it, unwinding a high-profile experiment that was once pitched as a long-term reserve plan and a path toward financial resilience.

💸 Sequans met fin à son pari Bitcoin

Sequans Communications tourne la page de sa stratégie crypto. L’entreprise française a vendu une partie de ses BTC pour rembourser sa dette convertible, clôturant ainsi son aventure Bitcoin.

Elle conserve 658 BTC, mais annonce ne plus…

— Cryptoast (@CryptoastMedia) May 28, 2026

The company confirmed it has fully redeemed its convertible debt by selling Bitcoin and now plans to monetize its remaining 658 BTC holdings. At its peak, Sequans held 3,234 BTC and positioned itself among a growing wave of public companies embracing Bitcoin as a corporate treasury asset.

The exit marks a dramatic reversal for a company that only months ago described Bitcoin as a cornerstone of its long-term strategy.

From Bitcoin Treasury Ambitions to Debt Repayment

Sequans launched its Bitcoin treasury initiative in June 2025 shortly after receiving a warning from the New York Stock Exchange that its market capitalization and shareholders' equity had fallen below required listing thresholds.

The company partnered with Swan Bitcoin and raised $384 million through a private placement, promoting the move as a way to strengthen its balance sheet and create long-term shareholder value.

But the structure of the financing carried significant risks from the start.

While $195 million came from equity issuance, another $189 million was raised through secured convertible debt backed by the Bitcoin itself. In effect, a large portion of the company's treasury strategy was financed using leverage tied directly to the value of its BTC holdings.

🔴 Bitcoin Price: $72,500 pic.twitter.com/QGOwcZ3bdW

— Bitcoin Price (@BTCPrice) May 28, 2026

By October 2025, Sequans had accumulated 3,234 BTC at an average purchase price of $116,643 per coin.

The strategy quickly came under pressure as market conditions deteriorated. Barely months after building its position, Sequans sold 970 BTC to meet debt obligations, becoming one of the first public Bitcoin treasury companies to liquidate a significant portion of its holdings.

The move contrasted sharply with the buy-and-hold philosophy championed by Strategy chairman Michael Saylor, who has consistently argued that corporate treasuries should avoid selling Bitcoin even during periods of market weakness.

A Costly Experiment Comes to an End

The company's latest announcement makes the company's stance crystal clear. In a statement, Sequans said

"Treasury Strategy Concluded."

Chief executive Georges Karam described the debt redemption as an important turning point and said the company is now fully focused on expanding its Internet of Things semiconductor business.

Sequans completes full redemption of its convertible debt ✅
Stronger balance sheet, simplified capital structure, and focus on scaling its IoT semiconductor business 🚀https://t.co/QYU5EPqjmO$SQNSpic.twitter.com/Nj46rs8yms

— Sequans (@Sequans) May 28, 2026

The tone represents a sharp departure from earlier statements praising Bitcoin as a premier long-term asset. Financial results help explain the change.

Sequans reported a $50.5 million operating loss during the first quarter of 2026 on revenue of just $6.1 million. Its 2025 annual report showed a net loss of $109.3 million, including a $67.4 million unrealized impairment tied to its Bitcoin holdings. The company's accumulated deficit reached $145.1 million.

JUST IN: Swedish public company Bitcoin Treasury Capital ($BTCB) buys an additional 1 #Bitcoin and now holds a total of 169.25 BTC.

🔸Bitcoin 100 Ranking: 92🔸 pic.twitter.com/phR8ewoJw3

— BitcoinTreasuries.NET (@BTCtreasuries) May 27, 2026

Meanwhile, Sequans shares have continued to slide. The stock has fallen roughly 77% over the past year and remains significantly below levels seen when the Bitcoin treasury strategy was first announced.

What was intended to strengthen the company's balance sheet ultimately became a costly detour. Less than a year after embracing Bitcoin as a long-term reserve asset, Sequans is now selling its remaining holdings and returning its focus to the semiconductor business that the strategy was supposed to help save.

data.title: How the Clarity Act is reshaping the stablecoin yield economy
data.description:
Author: @BlazingKevin_, Blockbooster Researcher

On May 14, 2026, the U.S. Senate Banking Committee passed the CLARITY Act by a bipartisan vote of 15-9.

The most important part of this "legislative progress" is Section 404 of the bill's text. This section, redrafted by Senators Thom Tillis and Angela Alsobrooks in a compromise text released on May 1, does two things that the GENIUS Act did not:

First, it extends the ban on stablecoin yields to all digital asset service providers (DASPs) and their affiliates—including centralized exchanges, brokers, dealers, and custodians. When the GENIUS Act was signed in July 2025, it only bound "stablecoin issuers" (PPSI/FPSI).

Coinbase, Anchorage Digital Neo Ltd., and others continued to offer users 3.5%-5% returns through a compliance loophole of "non-issuer interest payments," but all were shut down by Section 404. Secondly, Section 404 explicitly introduces a legal dichotomy between "passive returns vs. active rewards." It prohibits rewards that are "functionally or economically equivalent to bank deposit interest"—that is, returns automatically generated solely based on holding—but retains rewards "based on real activity or transactions," such as staking, market making, credit card cashback, and merchant transaction rewards. These two changes together constitute a paradigm shift. The stablecoin industry is moving from a market where holding pays interest to a market where using pays interest. Meanwhile, in the past month, the three largest asset management firms on Wall Street (Morgan Stanley, BlackRock, and JPMorgan) have almost simultaneously launched money market funds tailored to stablecoin reserve needs. Morgan Stanley's MSNXX was established on April 16th and publicly announced on April 23rd; BlackRock simultaneously filed two tokenized funds, BSTBL and BRSRV, on May 8th; and JPMorgan filed JLTXX on May 12th. These three companies launched products with highly similar functionalities almost simultaneously within 28 days. This timing is certainly not a coincidence. We believe that the anticipated passage of CLARITY Section 404 is pushing the stablecoin yield economy towards a new paradigm—the hold-to-earn path is narrowing, the use-to-earn path is being retained, and tokenized money market funds, as compliant interest-paying tools for stablecoin reserves, are becoming the most stable and profitable compliant yield layer in this new paradigm. The concentrated filing of products by Wall Street asset management giants in April and May is an industry positioning for this paradigm shift. It needs to be clarified that CLARITY has only passed the Senate Banking Committee and is still some distance from presidential signature, but the market expects it to be restructured in this direction. This article will start by reconstructing the timeline, dissecting the legal structure of the succession between GENIUS and CLARITY, and analyzing why the tokenized reserve asset layer has become the most stable compliant return channel in the new paradigm. 1. 30 Days of Industry Positioning 1.1 April 16: Morgan Stanley's Opening Move Let's go back to the earliest event. On April 16, 2026, Morgan Stanley's Stablecoin Reserves Portfolio (ticker: MSNXX) was officially launched. MSIM publicly announced the product on April 23. MSNXX's product positioning is very precise. The official statement reads: "This fund provides compliant stablecoin issuers with a qualified money market fund option, allowing them to invest in the reserve assets needed to back circulating stablecoins." MSNXX is a product tailored to reserve asset requirements—investing in cash, US Treasury bonds maturing within 93 days, and overnight repurchase agreements backed by Treasury bonds. However, MSNXX is not a tokenized product and is not traded on-chain. Morgan Stanley's product strategy is conservative—it only offers traditional MMF packaging, allowing stablecoin issuers to invest through traditional financial channels. This is the first publicly announced product among Wall Street asset management giants "specifically designed for stablecoin reserve needs." It's not revolutionary in itself, but it sends a clear signal: the demand for stablecoin reserves is so large that asset management giants are willing to set up a dedicated fund for it. 1.2 May 8: BlackRock's "Double Filing" 22 days later, BlackRock simultaneously filed two registration statements with the SEC: the tokenized version of the BlackRock Select Treasury Based Liquidity Fund (BSTBL) and the BlackRock Daily Reinvestment Stablecoin Reserve Vehicle (BRSRV). The design of these two products contrasts sharply with MSNXX. BSTBL is a tokenized version of BlackRock's existing Select Treasury Based Liquidity Fund. It serves traditional institutional cash managers—clients have already purchased this fund, now they simply have an additional on-chain distribution channel. BRSRV, on the other hand, is a newly created tokenized money market fund, distributed across multiple chains by Securitize, targeting only one client group: stablecoin issuers. The key difference between BlackRock and Morgan Stanley lies in tokenization. BlackRock chooses to issue the same asset (short-term Treasury bonds + cash + overnight repurchase agreements) to stablecoin issuers through on-chain tranches, giving the reserve assets themselves on-chain composability, 24/7 liquidity, and the potential for integration with DeFi protocols. This is a product form tailored for crypto-native clients (such as Ethena and Jupiter). The filing of BSTBL + BRSRV expands BlackRock's existing product portfolio, extending tokenized infrastructure from BUIDL's "DeFi collateral" use case to BRSRV's "stablecoin reserve asset" use case. 1.3 May 12: JPMorgan Chase's Second Entry Four days later, JPMorgan Chase filed for the JPMorgan OnChain Liquidity-Token Money Market Fund (JLTXX) with the SEC. The fund itself invests in US Treasury bonds and overnight repurchase agreements secured by Treasury bonds or cash, with underlying assets identical to BUIDL, BSTBL, and BRSRV. Token Class Shares are dated May 13.

LTXX is not JPMorgan's first on-chain MMF. Back on December 15, 2025, JPMorgan Asset Management launched the My OnChain Net Yield Fund (MONY) on Ethereum. MONY is a 506(c) private fund, open only to qualified investors.

This means JPMorgan has nearly five months of operational experience in the tokenized MMF field. JLTXX is not a catch-up product, but rather the second step in JPMorgan's on-chain MMF strategy—expanding the product, originally limited to 506(c) qualified investors, into a registered fund targeting a broader client base, specifically targeting the stablecoin reserve use case.

JPMorgan Chase is exploring the issuance of a syndicated stablecoin with Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and Citigroup in 2025, while simultaneously expanding its product portfolio through MONY → JLTXX to deeply invest in the tokenized reserve asset sector. Regardless of the OCC's final ruling, JPMorgan Chase will have a product in place—this "hedging" is a unique strategic advantage for JPMorgan Chase as a GSIB bank and asset management company. 1.4 May 14: The CLARITY Act Endorses the Entire Sector On May 14, the Senate Banking Committee passed the CLARITY Act with a bipartisan vote of 15-9. It's worth noting that Morgan Stanley's MSNXX, BlackRock's BSTBL/BRSRV, and JPMorgan Chase's JLTXX—these products were all in preparation before the CLARITY Section 404 compromise text was made public. In fact, since CLARITY was first shelved in January 2026, the asset management industry has been clear on two things: First, the "hold-to-earn" stablecoin reward path will eventually be closed. Second, stablecoin reserve assets must exist, must be compliant, and must pay interest. Combining these two points: when the hold-to-earn path is narrowed, one of the most robust "indirect income" transmission paths is through the reserve asset layer—the stablecoin issuer itself does not pay interest, but its tokenized money market fund legally pays interest to the issuer, and the issuer decides how to pass this income to users within a compliant framework. The products of asset management giants are the infrastructure prepared for this "most stable compliant return channel". 2. Why CLARITY is much more important than GENIUS 2.1 The limited scope of the GENIUS Act To understand the paradigm shift effect of Section 404, we must first precisely understand the object of its extension—GENIUS Act 4(a)(11). The GENIUS Act, signed into force in July 2025, stipulates that compliant stablecoin issuers or foreign stablecoin issuers may not pay any form of interest or income to stablecoin holders. In other words, the GENIUS Act itself does not distinguish between "passive income" and "activity rewards." All forms of interest or income paid by the issuer to the holder are prohibited. Secondly, its constraints only apply to the issuer itself, excluding exchanges, wallets, custodians, affiliates, and other third parties. This second restriction creates a regulatory loophole—known in the industry as a "pass-through evasion." The stablecoin industry throughout 2025-2026 is essentially searching for compliant innovation within this loophole: The Coinbase/Kraken model: Exchanges distribute rewards. USDC is issued by Circle, but Coinbase distributes approximately 4% of the rewards to USDC holders through the Coinbase One subscription model. Gemini Credit Card Model: Rewards are triggered through transactions with external merchants. GUSD is issued by Gemini Trust Company, but Gemini credit card holders receive GUSD cashback when making purchases at merchants. Anchorage Digital Neo Model: Payments are made through an independently affiliated legal entity. USDtb is issued by Anchorage Digital Bank, but rewards are paid by Anchorage Digital Neo Ltd. (an independent legal entity). These three models together form the "indirect interest payment" ecosystem of the GENIUS era. However, the compliance basis for all of this is that the GENIUS Act only governs the limited scope of issuers. 2.2 Substantive Extension of CLARITY Section 404 The CLARITY Act Section 404 did two things that the GENIUS Act did not. First: Extension to DASPs and Affiliates Section 404 no longer only applies to stablecoin issuers, but extends to "covered digital asset service providers and their affiliates." This scope explicitly covers centralized exchanges, brokers, dealers, and custodians. This extension immediately shuts down all compliance paths for "paying interest through non-issuers," including Coinbase, Kraken, Gemini, and Anchorage Digital Neo. Coinbase, as a DASP, can no longer pay hold-only USDC rewards; Anchorage Digital Neo can no longer pay USDtb rewards. The second thing: Introducing the "Passive vs. Active" dichotomy. Section 404 prohibits DASPs from providing returns that are "functionally or economically equivalent to bank deposit interest," but retains rewards "based on real activity or transactions." This means that any reward tied to "consumption, trading, staking, or transfer" can survive, but any reward that grows linearly with idle balance cannot. These two things together constitute a complete paradigm shift. All "indirect interest payment" templates from the GENIUS era will either be shut down or need to be redesigned in the CLARITY era. The stablecoin industry is shifting from a market where holding earns interest to a market where using earns interest. 2.3 Winner Paths in Paradigm Shift Under the use-to-earn paradigm, there are three possible paths to pass on rewards to users. Path A: Redesigning Rewards as Activity-Based Rewards Applicable to: Exchanges, wallets, credit cards. Coinbase may change USDC rewards from "holding earns" to "based on transaction frequency/spending amount." Gemini is already using a credit card cashback model. The key issue is not whether Path A can retain users, but its design cost—Coinbase needs to reconstruct the legal framework and product UI of the entire rewards system, and every active design must undergo fact-checking by the SEC/CFTC. This reconstruction requires 6-12 months. In the short term, user churn is a real risk. However, in the medium term, Path A could very well recover or even surpass the appeal of the hold-to-earn era. Path B: Keeping yields at the protocol layer and delivering them to users through activity-based operations. Applicable to: DeFi protocols. Section 404's definition of "covered digital asset service providers" is explicitly built around centralized intermediaries—yields generated by non-custodial smart contracts—such as supplying USDC to Aave for variable-rate lending—are not by design within this definition. This means that users depositing USDC into Aave lending pools to obtain variable interest rates is currently considered compliant by most legal scholars—CLARITY seemingly inadvertently provides a yield channel for non-custodial DeFi. However, this exemption carries significant uncertainty. If the final rule extends the concept of "economic equivalence" to non-custodial DeFi, or... The front end is defined as a related party, and the exemption for Path B may be substantially narrowed. Path C: Paying interest through the reserve asset layer. This is the path that Wall Street asset management giants are betting on. Specific mechanism: Stablecoin issuers themselves do not pay interest, nor do DASPs, but the reserve assets of stablecoins are tokenized money market funds, which legally pay interest to their holders (i.e., stablecoin issuers). After receiving the returns distributed by the fund, the stablecoin issuer retains them as company profits—or partially passes them on to users through active behavior rewards. The key compliance advantage of this path: its return layer is not at the stablecoin layer, nor at the DASP layer, but at the underlying fund layer—unrelated to the stablecoin regulatory framework. These three paths are not mutually exclusive but will evolve synchronously. Path A might find new life in the hands of players like Coinbase who possess retail brands and distribution channels; Path B might unexpectedly benefit protocols like Aave and Pendle (but comes with tail risks of regulatory tightening over the next 12 months); Path C is the least directly threatened by Section 404, but requires the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency's 20% cap to be rejected as a prerequisite. Path C is the "most robust beneficiary" compliant yield layer, but not the "only beneficiary." This is why Wall Street asset management giants concentrated on registering tokenized money market funds in April and May. They are providing one compliant yield infrastructure for the use-to-earn paradigm that is about to be finalized under CLARITY Section 404. Considering the implementation costs and regulatory uncertainties of Path A and B respectively, Path C... The most attractive option after risk adjustment—this is BlackRock's industry assessment. 2.4 The Collaborative Relationship Between Path B and Path C Path B and Path C seem to have collaborative potential. A complete on-chain yield system can utilize two paths simultaneously: The reserve asset layer uses BUIDL—ensuring the source of compliant yields. The user layer uses Aave lending or Pendle yield splitting—ensuring that the "yields" perceived by users come from active operations. This "BUIDL" at the underlying DeFi level... The two-layer structure of "the protocol on the surface" can theoretically build a compliant and user-friendly use-to-earn system. BlackRock clearly didn't specifically foresee Section 404 when it launched BUIDL, but this product happens to be the best underlying layer for a use-to-earn system under the new paradigm.

3. BlackRock's Three-Layer Product Matrix—Infrastructure Built for the New Paradigm

3.1 Three Products, Three Customer Groups

To understand BlackRock's strategy, its three tokenized fund products need to be compared side-by-side:

  • BUIDL: 2024

    Launched in March, natively built on Ethereum. Legal structure: BVI fund, custody provided by Securitize. Target customers: DeFi protocols, crypto-native institutions, and on-chain scenarios requiring BUIDL as collateral. Accepted as eligible collateral on lending protocols such as Aave, with a minimum investment of $5 million. BSTBL: Filed May 8, 2026. Legal structure: Government money market fund registered with the US SEC, with BNY Mellon Investment Servicing acting as the transfer agent. Target customers: Traditional institutional cash managers—clients already using BlackRock funds, now gaining 24/7 trading capabilities through on-chain shares. BSRV: 2026 Filed on May 8th. The legal structure is a newly created money market fund, Securitize multi-chain distribution. Target customers: Stablecoin issuers – tailored for compliant reserve needs under the GENIUS Act. These are three products existing simultaneously in the market, but with completely non-overlapping customer groups. This tiered product matrix design: the same underlying asset (short-term government bonds + cash + overnight repurchase agreements), sold to three entirely different customer groups through different legal packaging, different custody structures, and different distribution channels. More importantly, these three products together constitute a complete tokenized reserve asset ecosystem, covering all needs under the use-to-earn paradigm: BUIDL as collateral and portfolio assets at the DeFi protocol layer; BSTBL as an on-chain cash management tool for traditional institutions; and BRSRV as the core underlying asset at the stablecoin issuer reserve asset layer. Regardless of use-to-earn... BlackRock has already prepared corresponding products regarding the specific design of the system and the required tokenized reserve assets. 4. 90% Concentration – A Neglected Systemic Risk in the CLARITY Paradigm Shift Next, we quantify the current concentration risk of BlackRock's BUIDL. When USDtb launched on December 16, 2024, the official cooperation announcement between Ethena and BlackRock clearly stated: "BUIDL accounts for over 90% of USDtb reserves. This is the largest allocation of any stablecoin to BUIDL." After JupUSD launched on January 6, 2026, its reserve structure was 90% USDtb + 10% USDC liquidity buffer. The concentration calculated from this is: BUIDL A single fund supports approximately 90% of USDtb's reserves and indirectly supports approximately 81% of JupUSD's reserves (90% of USDtb × 90% of JupUSD). USDtb's historical peak circulating supply was approximately $1.2 billion (June 2025 data), and JupUSD's size has grown rapidly since its launch in January 2026. This means that the health of the BUIDL single fund directly determines the solvency of at least two important stablecoins. If BUIDL experiences large-scale redemption pressure, the reserve assets of downstream USDtb and JupUSD will simultaneously become invalid. The CLARITY paradigm shift further amplifies this concentration risk. 5. The Game of the OCC 20% Reserve Asset Cap—Determining Which Path A, B, or C Wins. 5.1 Cap Proposal and Opposition On March 2nd, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) published a 376-page proposal in the Federal Register as part of the implementation details of the Genius Act. One proposal sparked industry-wide discussion: as a possible alternative threshold, the OCC explored whether to set a 20% cap on the proportion of "tokenized assets" in the reserve assets of Federally Chartered Stablecoin Issuers (PFSIs). Although this was only the OCC raising 20% ​​as a possible option during its consultation process, market participants have already interpreted this alternative threshold as a strong signal of regulatory intent. If this cap is implemented, it means that PPSIs can only place a maximum of 20% of their reserve assets in tokenized funds (such as BUIDL, JLTXX, BRSRV), while the remaining 80% must be placed in traditional non-tokenized assets. If this 20% cap is passed, it will directly impact the scalability of the tokenized reserve asset layer. 5.2 This is a zero-sum game that determines the outcome of each path. The true meaning of the OCC 20% cap is that it is the key variable determining whether path C can be scaled in the three yield channels of path A, B, and C in the CLARITY paradigm shift. Supporters of the cap include JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and Citigroup—who announced in 2025 that they would explore the possibility of jointly issuing stablecoins. If the 20% cap is passed, 80% of PPSI reserve assets must be placed in traditional assets, meaning that most reserve funds will flow back into the bank deposit system, with these four banks being the biggest beneficiaries. Opponents of the cap include BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street. Asset management giants. If the cap is removed or significantly relaxed, PPSI reserve assets can be 100% placed in tokenized money market funds (including BUIDL, BSTBL, BRSRV), with these asset management companies being the biggest beneficiaries. Path C is fully open.

    5.3 Game Changes After the Passage of CLARITY

    On May 14, the CLARITY bill passed the Senate Banking Committee, adding a key variable to the OCC 20% cap game.

    The CLARITY bill provides a clear legal status for tokenized securities—this indirectly weakens the OCC's argument that "tokenized assets have special risks and require additional restrictions." If CLARITY grants legal status to tokenized funds, the OCC Using the "inherent risks of tokenization" as a reason for restriction is untenable. With the CLARITY + GENIUS framework in place, OCC is expected to have to adjust its 20% alternative threshold. The most likely outcome is that the threshold will be abolished or significantly relaxed. This would be a partial victory for BlackRock's preferred "principles-based" approach. However, we must confront a crucial issue: Path C's scaling victory and the systemic risks of concentration discussed in Part 4 are two sides of the same coin. If the OCC's 20% threshold is relaxed, BUIDL-like foundations could rapidly absorb tens or even hundreds of billions of dollars in stablecoin reserves, realizing the industrial value bet by BlackRock and others. But simultaneously, the risks of single-point failures in BUIDL, reflexive stampedes, and the "pyramid-shaped concentration" risk in the crypto-dollar economy would all be amplified. In other words, Path C... The victory of Path C is a victory for BlackRock in an industrial sense, but in a systemic sense, it marks the birth of a new type of concentration risk. Traditional finance uses SIFMU certification, CCAR stress testing, and DTCC disaster recovery mechanisms to manage this large-scale concentration. Currently, the on-chain tokenized reserve asset layer has no equivalent mechanism; therefore, if Path C's victory occurs, it will be accompanied by a window of opportunity—a window for the regulatory framework to catch up with concentration risk. Whether the FSOC will begin to intervene in this concentration issue in 2027-2028 is a policy variable worth tracking. Conclusion: The entire stablecoin yield economy has been forcibly reset from "holding for interest" to "using for interest," and tokenized money market funds, as underlying reserve assets, have become one of the most robust and beneficial compliant yield infrastructures in this new paradigm. The product layout of Wall Street asset management giants—MSIM's MSNXX, BlackRock's... BSTBL/BRSRV and JPMorgan's JLTXX are examples of companies positioning themselves for this paradigm shift. The true protagonists in this direction are the tokenized money market fund providers at the very bottom of the value chain. Visa and Mastercard don't directly face consumers, but they've built a high-margin, high-growth, and highly competitive business model by charging approximately 0.1-0.3% network fees per transaction—their combined market capitalization exceeds $1 trillion, far surpassing most credit card issuers. Tokenized reserve asset providers (BlackRock, JPMorgan Chase, Morgan Stanley) are playing the same role in the crypto-dollar economy. What we are witnessing is a regulatory-driven reshuffling of the financial infrastructure layer. The CLARITY Act closed the "indirect interest payment" path of the GENIUS era, but it didn't close the returns themselves—the returns were forcibly repositioned at the reserve asset layer. The new world's Visa and Mastercard are already in place.

data.title: Viral Thai Police Photo Showing Officers In Glittery Dresses Confirmed As AI Fake Posted By Police
data.description:
Thai police officers posing in glittery stage dresses beside a handcuffed drug suspect became one of the internet’s most shared law enforcement images this week.

The unusual scene sparked headlines across the world, with reports claiming officers had disguised themselves as female dancers to carry out a covert raid during a street festival in Lopburi province.

But the viral image that fuelled the story was not real.

Police later admitted the widely circulated photograph had been generated using artificial intelligence, even though the anti-drug operation itself genuinely took place.

AI Image Fooled Global Media

The image first appeared on the Facebook page of Tha Luang Provincial Police Station in Lopburi province, Thailand.

It showed six officers, including five men in sequinned dresses and feathered costumes, standing around a suspect after an alleged drug arrest.

International tabloids and news outlets quickly picked up the story, describing how officers supposedly went undercover as dancers to surprise the suspect during local celebrations.

However, AFP later traced the image back to the police station and determined there was a “very high” likelihood it had been created using AI tools.

Police sergeant Rachata Mitrsuripong, who manages the station’s Facebook page, later confirmed the image was fake.

He told AFP,

“I wanted to create a friendlier image of the police – showing a cute and humorous side – so that people would feel more comfortable approaching officers.”

Rachata also shared the original arrest photo, which showed five male officers in ordinary clothing surrounding the suspect.

The woman in the colourful dancer outfit seen in the viral image did not exist in the original photograph.

He added that the arrest details shared online were genuine, except for the suspect’s published name.

Real Drug Raid Behind The Fake Picture

Although the AI-generated image captured global attention, authorities said the underlying operation was real.

Police reportedly arrested a suspect during a festival operation in Tha Luang district, Lopburi province, over alleged drug trafficking and illegal online gambling activities.

Officers seized 53 methamphetamine tablets, more than 200 small plastic bags believed to have been prepared for drug distribution, and a mobile phone allegedly connected to online slot machine operations.

The suspect is facing charges for possessing category 1 narcotics with intent to sell, as well as operating an unauthorised online gambling platform.

Authorities said he remains in custody while investigations continue.

After the arrest, officers posed for photographs with the suspect, including the altered AI version that later spread online.

Police joked on social media after the image went viral,

“We didn't want to just go out and arrest him; that would be a waste of money on costume rentals.”

Thailand’s Unusual Undercover Operations Keep Going Viral

The bizarre image gained traction partly because Thailand’s police force has already built a reputation for unconventional undercover tactics.

Earlier in 2026, officers from Bangkok Metropolitan Police disguised themselves as performers inside a lion dance troupe during Lunar New Year celebrations to arrest a suspected repeat thief at a temple fair.

Police captain Lertvarit Lertvorapreecha told The Guardian,

“The dance was completely improvised. We just did what we could.”

According to reports, one officer accidentally grabbed a male lion dance mask while dressing up, pairing it with a red silk costume and tactical boots.

🇹🇭 LMAO - BEST UNDERCOVER OPERATION!

Thailand’s Metropolitan Cops hid in a traditional two-person lion costume and danced… to catch a criminal!

The wanted burglar didn’t expect it and was caught off guard… as the police officers jumped out of the lion suit. pic.twitter.com/vIRUKWwoTy

— Lord Bebo (@MyLordBebo) February 21, 2026

Children reportedly joined the performance while passersby laughed, leaving the suspect unaware that police were approaching.

Thai officers have also previously disguised themselves as construction workers, wrestlers and even men covered in bushes and dried leaves during undercover operations targeting suspects linked to rape, child sexual abuse and drug crimes.

Police say the disguises help them catch suspects off guard.

According to officers involved in past operations, the easiest way to arrest someone is while they are distracted and “enjoying life and unaware of what's happening around them”.

One widely discussed case involved an officer camouflaged in dried leaves crawling through rice fields for hundreds of metres to approach a suspect accused of sexually assaulting a minor.

A Thai police officer donned a full ghillie suit and crawled through rice fields to catch a sexual assault suspect hiding under a house in Chachoengsao province.

The creative arrest method has gone viral on social media and drawn widespread attention.

🧵1 pic.twitter.com/QIHaoQY3CA

— BFM News (@NewsBFM) January 2, 2025

Fake Police Photo Exposes Growing AI Verification Problem

The incident has also highlighted how rapidly AI-generated images are blurring the line between reality and fiction, even when shared from official sources.

Several international publications, including tabloids in the UK and the United States, initially reported the image as authentic before later clarifying that it had been digitally generated.

Editors and fact-checkers increasingly face difficulties verifying viral content as AI-generated imagery becomes harder to detect.

The situation becomes even more complicated when manipulated visuals are distributed through seemingly trustworthy accounts, including official organisations and government agencies.

While some viewers immediately questioned the authenticity of the extravagant costumes, others accepted the image because it originated from a police Facebook page.

Media organisations are now facing pressure from both sides, trying to avoid publishing fake AI visuals while also responding to audiences who wrongly accuse genuine photographs of being artificially generated.

data.title: The rise of USD-denominated stablecoins and the evolution of on-chain foreign exchange infrastructure
data.description:
Author: Gate Ventures

Introduction

When stablecoins are mentioned, the market usually thinks of USD stablecoins such as USDT and USDC first. This is not surprising, because data shows that USD stablecoins still reach nearly 99% of the market supply and have long served as the main trading currency, settlement unit, and liquidity infrastructure foundation of the crypto market.

However, non-USD stablecoins are gradually showing noteworthy development momentum. From January 2023 to February 2026, the total supply of local currency stablecoins increased from approximately $700 million to nearly $1.2 billion, an increase of about 70%; more noteworthy is that their on-chain circulation scale increased from approximately $600 million to approximately $10 billion, achieving an increase of about 16 times. (1) In this trend, the positioning of currency value is undergoing structural changes. The function is gradually evolving from a crypto liquidity tool centered on the US dollar to a broader multi-currency financial infrastructure. In this process, non-USD stablecoins and on-chain FX are gradually forming a complementary relationship and beginning to reconstruct the architectural foundation of cross-border payments, corporate fund management, and local currency settlement. Meanwhile, infrastructure platforms like Circle StableFX have emerged, specifically designed to support on-chain foreign exchange, providing exchange and liquidity routing capabilities between different stablecoin ecosystems through an RFQ exchange mechanism. Currently, the non-USD stablecoin market is still primarily concentrated in the Euro and Ruble, but their growth logics differ. For Euro stablecoins, their advantages mainly stem from the regulatory certainty provided by MiCA and the Euro's status as a major global currency. MiCA's compliance framework reduces institutional equity, making it easier for Euro stablecoins like the Euro to enter payment, DeFi, fund management, and on-chain foreign exchange scenarios, gradually evolving into on-chain Euro financial infrastructure. In contrast, the demand for the Ruble as a stable local currency is more limited by border payments and geopolitical factors. Especially after the Russia-Ukraine conflict, the Russian financial system faces problems such as SWIFT network access restrictions, a contraction in dollar settlement channels, and decreased efficiency in border payments, significantly disrupting traditional international flows. In this context, the value of ruble stablecoins lies more in providing a more flexible on-chain settlement method for regional trade and defined payment scenarios, rather than serving DeFi or on-chain yield scenarios. On the other hand, we also see more and more countries promoting the development of their own currency stablecoins; for example, Japan and South Korea are actively exploring relevant frameworks. This round of development is largely driven by regulation: countries hope to introduce their own currencies into the on-chain financial system based on public issuance, redemption, and compliance requirements. (2) (3) As more sovereign currencies are tokenized and enter on-chain circulation, an early on-chain foreign exchange market is taking shape. In the future, different local stablecoins will no longer be transferred solely through USD stablecoins, but will have the opportunity to achieve direct conversion through on-chain liquidity, market makers, and atomic pool settlement mechanisms, thereby gradually building a multi-currency, real-time on-chain foreign exchange settlement network. Why are non-USD stablecoins needed? If USDT/USDC already accounts for nearly 99% of the market, why are local currency stablecoins still needed? The emergence of USD stablecoins is not intended to directly challenge the liquidity status of USD stablecoins, but rather to address the structural shortcomings of USD stablecoins in local currency settlement, cross-border payments, and corporate fund management. While USD stablecoins solve the liquidity problem in the global crypto market, in real-world business scenarios, businesses, merchants, and individuals ultimately still need to receive, pay, record, and settle transactions in their own currencies. Therefore, when fund flows involve different countries, currencies, and banks, relying solely on USD stablecoins cannot completely eliminate issues such as foreign exchange conversion, deposits and withdrawals, prepayments, and local consistency. The value of local currency stablecoins lies in directly introducing the local currency onto the blockchain, allowing businesses and users to retain exposure in their own currency while gaining the advantage of real-time and cross-border liquidity for settlement. EURC / BRLA / XSGD Case Studies: Development Paths in Different Regions USD-denominated stablecoins do not develop along a single path, but are determined by local regulatory environments, payment pain points, and local financial infrastructure. EURC, BRLA, and XSGD, as stablecoins with different development paths, represent different typical models: regulatory-driven on-chain Euro infrastructure, real-payment-driven corporate settlement tools, and an invisible settlement layer behind regional payment networks. The Eurozone's development has primarily benefited from the gradual declaration of the European regulatory framework. MiCA provides clear rules for stablecoin issuance, reserve management, redemption mechanisms, and compliance requirements, reducing the need for deposits by financial institutions, payment companies, and DeFi protocols to adopt European stablecoins. Meanwhile, the euro itself is one of the world's major international currencies, with a broad base of use in trade, bonds, and foreign exchange reserves. Therefore, when the euro is introduced onto the blockchain, it naturally possesses the conditions to become an on-chain euro settlement asset. In terms of payments, EURC has already established connections with traditional payment networks such as Visa. Through real-time payment and clearing infrastructures such as Visa Direct, EURC can be used for faster cross-border payments, corporate settlements, and multi-currency fund transfers, enabling the euro to enter existing payment networks on the blockchain, rather than remaining confined to encrypted scenarios.

At the DeFi level, EURC has also begun to enter mainstream network markets such as Aave and Morpho. On Aave, EURC can be used as a network asset or collateralized asset, providing users with on-chain network scenarios denominated in euros; on Morpho, EURC aggregates liquidity and optimizes interest rates through Vaults managed by professional institutions, enabling institutional and high-level users to allocate euro funds more efficiently.

Therefore, while most non-USD stablecoins are still confined to payment or regional settlement models, EURC is closer to the development path of USDC: it is not only a payment asset but is also entering payment networks, leveraged markets, fund management chains, and on-FX scenarios, gradually transforming into on-chain Euro financial infrastructure. The demand from multiple scenarios and broader infrastructure integration have also driven EURC's continuous and stable growth since its issuance, with its total supply now exceeding $500 million. The logic behind BRLA is significantly different. Brazil and Latin America have long faced problems such as high costs for cross-border payments, large foreign exchange spreads, local currency fluctuations, and low efficiency in bank settlements. While Brazil already has a highly mature instant payment network, PIX, it primarily addresses real-time transfers between accounts within Brazil. When funds need to leave Brazil and enter other monetary systems, or for cross-border supplier payments, overseas payroll, and regional asset transfers, businesses still rely on banks, foreign exchange brokers, correspondent banks, and traditional cross-border networks; existing cross-border payment discrepancies have not been completely eliminated. The value of BRLA is being built: it is not a replacement for PIX, but rather connects local payment networks like PIX to on-chain settlement systems. Through BRLA, BRL funds can enter the blockchain from the local banking system and then be exchanged and settled with USDC, EURC, or other local stablecoins, thereby supporting more efficient cross-border business fund flows.

Therefore, BRLA's core value is not to provide liquidity for DeFi, but to extend efficient Brazilian payments to cross-border and on-chain financial scenarios. With the increase in actual payments and corporate cash flow needs, BRLA's on-chain transaction volume has continued to climb, surpassing $400 million in February 2026.

Besides payments, BRLA is gradually becoming an important part of on-chain foreign exchange and liquidity management. Its average monthly trading volume on DEX is approximately $10 million, more than 10 times higher than at the beginning of 2024.

BRLA's asset scenarios are also supported by Circle's foreign exchange infrastructure, including StableFX and the emerging Arc ecosystem.

BRLA's asset scenarios are also supported by Circle's foreign exchange infrastructure, including StableFX and the emerging Arc ecosystem.

First, at the liquidity layer, StableFX does not adopt the traditional AMM model, but instead uses the RFQ (Request For Quote) mechanism.

After a user submits a swap request, the institutional market maker immediately returns a quote. This means that liquidity no longer depends on the size of on-chain pools, but can directly access traditional forex liquidity sources such as OTC, banks, and large brokers.

This model is particularly important for non-USD currency pairs such as BRL, SGD, and EUR, because on-chain liquidity usually needs to support large-scale transactions. (6) Secondly, StableFX attempts to solve one of the biggest pain points in cross-border payments: settlement risk. In the past, cross-border foreign exchange transactions generally had the following: A pays first → waits for B to pay → assumes counterparty risk. StableFX introduces an on-chain PvP mechanism, which uses smart escrow to manage funds. Settlement is only executed when both parties have delivered their funds; otherwise, the transaction is automatically cancelled. In other words, it attempts to bring the logic of traditional CLS (Continuous Linked Settlement) onto the blockchain. In addition, Circle has also launched a partner stablecoin mechanism, hoping to incorporate the local stablecoin issuance system into a unified framework. Access parties need to meet the following requirements: Local regulatory approval; 1:1 value-added assets; Automatic minting/redemption; Bank connectivity; Regular audits and compliance requirements. If local stablecoins such as HKD, BRL, and SGD are integrated in the future, more foreign exchange trading corridors can be gradually established. If on-chain foreign exchange deviates from the traditional foreign exchange market in price, it may attract arbitrage funds, forming a one-sided arbitrage flow; if market makers cannot hedge in time, they may widen spreads, reduce quote sizes, or even suspend quotes.

For example, suppose in the traditional forex market: USD/BRL = 5.00

But on-chain, due to insufficient BRLA liquidity: USDC/BRLA = 5.20

Arbitrageurs can: Buy BRL on TradeFi → Sell BRLA on-chain → Wide spread

If market makers are not fast enough at hedging, or there is insufficient BRLA inventory, they will be continuously exploited by arbitrage funds.

The result is that market makers begin to: widen spreads → reduce quote sizes → reduce liquidity funding. The final market phenomenon may be: excessive price increases, decreased depth, and even situations where quotes are available but cannot be executed during periods of volatility. Therefore, the core challenge of on-chain forex is not just "whether there is liquidity," but how to effectively connect TradeFi's liquidity, risk management, and on-chain settlement. This also raises a crucial question: If on-chain liquidity is insufficient, why can't stablecoins be issued directly and instantly? Theoretically, it's possible, but in reality, minting is not a purely on-chain operation. Taking EURC as an example, new issuance typically still requires institutions to complete KYC, submit funds through a Circle minting account, and transfer Wire/SEPA euros from various banks to the issuer's account. Only after the funds are transferred on-chain will the issuer mint the corresponding amount of EURC on-chain. Therefore, market makers cannot simply "replenish their inventory out of thin air" the instant a transaction occurs. Their more common practice is to hold inventory positions in advance, hedge risks immediately through the traditional foreign exchange market, and then replenish stablecoin inventory subsequently through banks and issuer channels. The future of on-chain forex will inevitably rely on spot local stablecoins. According to BIS data, approximately 31% of forex market transactions come from spot trading, and approximately 69% come from forex swaps, forwards, options, and other derivative instruments. This means that the modern foreign exchange market is more about transferring exchange rate risk than relying on real global currency settlement. (7) This means that in addition to spot FX paths such as USDC ↔ EURC / BRLA, on-chain foreign exchange may also develop a synthetic FX model: Users continue to hold USDC / USDT at the underlying level, and obtain exposure in local currencies such as EUR, BRL, CHF, and SGD through NDF (non-deliverable forward). The account balance is denominated in local currency, but the underlying settlement is still in USD stablecoins. This advantage is that it retains the deep liquidity of USDT / USDC, does not require the re-establishment of spot liquidity for each stablecoin, and also reduces the basic dependence on local banks, reserve assets, and minting/redemption. Therefore, the future of on-chain foreign exchange may have two exit paths: one is spot foreign exchange for real payments and local settlement, and the other is synthetic foreign exchange for multi-currency accounts, treasury management, and risk hedging. Looking ahead, the growth trend of stablecoins as a means of payment and value transfer is becoming increasingly apparent. For them to truly scale, native currency stablecoins and on-chain foreign exchange are likely to be an essential link. This trend has already begun to emerge in Latin America. An increasing number of new crypto banks are integrating native currencies, stablecoins, and native payment networks. For example, in the Brazilian and Argentinian markets, scenarios like USDC ↔ BRLA / USDC ↔ PEZO have emerged, with integration into local payment systems: BRLA → PIX → Brazilian bank account. For users, the experience is: USD stablecoin → local currency → local payment. However, the essence is: on-chain forex → local stablecoin → local payment network. If regulations continue to improve, market maker liquidity matures, and infrastructure like Circle StableFX integrates more local currencies, the long-term opportunity for on-chain forex will not be sufficient to serve cryptocurrency trading, but rather to become part of the global cross-border payment infrastructure.

data.title: OKX Ventures, in conjunction with South Korean investment institutions, makes a strategic investment in Coinone.
data.description: On May 29, Coinone, South Korea's leading virtual asset exchange, announced that it has formally signed a strategic equity investment agreement with Com2uS Holdings and its affiliate, Korea Investment & Securities (KIS), and OKX Ventures, the investment arm of global fintech and cryptocurrency company OKX. According to the agreement, KIS and OKX Ventures will each invest 80 billion won (approximately US$53 million), and upon completion of relevant regulatory approvals, will each hold 19.6% of Coinone's shares, becoming the joint third-largest shareholder, after Coinone CEO Cha Myung-hoon (holding 27.8%) and Com2uS Holdings and its affiliates (holding 25.0%). This investment will be completed through a combination of acquiring secondary market shares from existing shareholders Mr. Cha Myung-hoon and Com2uS Holdings, and subscribing to newly issued shares. Following the investment, Mr. Cha Myung-hoon is expected to remain Coinone's largest shareholder, and his management control will remain unaffected. Com2uS Holdings stated that it will further deepen its collaboration with partners possessing both the high credibility of licensed financial institutions and world-class infrastructure to generate synergies and achieve multi-party value growth. KIS plans to align with South Korean legislative trends and collaborate with Coinone to advance security token offerings (STOs) and stablecoin businesses, jointly exploring new financial models that integrate regulated finance and virtual assets. Simultaneously, both parties will leverage the expertise of financial institutions in asset management and operations, combined with the blockchain infrastructure advantages of virtual asset exchanges, to explore new synergies in the institutional and derivatives markets. Furthermore, both parties have reached a consensus to jointly enhance Coinone's market credibility and transaction security by sharing expertise in compliance areas such as anti-money laundering (AML) and suspicious transaction detection. Kim Sung-hwan, CEO of Korea Investment & Securities, stated, "Through this strategic equity investment in Coinone, we will actively contribute to building a robust virtual asset ecosystem while accelerating the cultivation of new growth engines in the virtual asset sector." Simultaneously, through its strategic partnership with OKX Ventures, Coinone expects to gain global market insights, international operational experience, and best practices in the digital asset industry. The two parties will also engage in in-depth discussions on core issues such as user protection, operational resilience, security, and risk management, helping Coinone continuously enhance its overall competitiveness in the institutional and corporate investor markets. Netero Dai, Vice President of Global Markets at OKX, stated, “South Korea is one of the world’s most mature digital asset markets, and its regulatory framework enjoys a high reputation in the international community. We firmly believe that the future financial system will be built on compliant and regulated infrastructure, and this joint investment in Coinone with Korea Investment & Securities is a concrete practice of this philosophy. We look forward to working closely with Coinone and Korea Investment & Securities to create the next generation of compliant digital financial services for Korean investors.” Myung-Hoon Cha, CEO of Coinone, stated, “We have carefully selected the best partners in each segment to achieve the greatest synergy with Coinone. This investment will strongly promote our leading position in the emerging field of blockchain-based digital financial infrastructure.” He further stated, “As a licensed operator, we will help the virtual asset industry gain social trust by building a responsible equity structure. At the same time, we will strictly comply with all relevant procedures and maintain close communication with financial regulatory agencies to ensure the smooth processing of reports on changes in major shareholders.” In addition, Coinone, Com2uS Holdings, Korea Investment & Securities, and OKX plan to jointly hold a press conference in June to fully explain the background, objectives, and expected impact of this strategic equity investment. Specific arrangements will be announced separately. About Coinone – “Bringing Blockchain to the World” Coinone is a virtual asset service provider registered in South Korea and one of the country's leading professional virtual asset exchanges. With the vision of “Bringing Blockchain to the World,” Coinone is committed to becoming a global leader in the digital asset and blockchain field. About OKX Ventures and OKX OKX Ventures is the investment arm of OKX. OKX is a global fintech company dedicated to modernizing money and markets. Currently, over 120 million users worldwide trust OKX to invest, trade, and transfer digital assets through its platform, which covers a wide range of financial instruments including spot, options, and decentralized markets. As one of the world's largest digital asset platforms, OKX is renowned for its exchange, wallet, and on-chain ecosystem, which are highly favored by individual users and large institutions. OKX has regional offices in the United States, Europe, the United Arab Emirates, and Singapore, and local offices in São Paulo, Hong Kong, Turkey, and Australia, having built one of the most comprehensive and compliant fintech company systems globally.
